observations.recommendation:
1.6 We recommended, and the City Accountant agreed, to ensure the timely
remittance of withheld taxes to the BIR, in strict compliance with the deadlines
prescribed under the BIR RR No. 11-2018.

2.33 We recommended, and the City Treasurer agreed, to:
a) Avoid any transfer of AFs among collectors in compliance with Section
25 of COA Circular No. 92-382;
b) Ensure that designation orders for AOs include specific duties,
responsibilities, and accountabilities, particularly regarding the proper
utilization, recording, and safekeeping of AFs;
c) Require the LO to maintain the Permanent Record Book of AFs and
ensure that all columns are properly filled out, with remarks on the
status of AFs for proper monitoring;
d) Require the RPT Collector to:
i. investigate the identified collection discrepancy and immediately
deposit the under-remittance;
ii. be more meticulous in managing AFs and ensure that the word
"CANCELLED" is clearly written on the face of any OR that is no
longer usable or valid;
iii. make the necessary adjustments for the errors noted in the reports;
iv. exercise diligence in recording and reconciling transactions to
prevent recurring errors and ensure the completeness of reports;
v. include the monthly certification at the end of the month in the
cashbook, duly signed by the AO
2.34 We also recommended, and the management agreed, to revisit the plan of
implementing an online payment facility to alleviate the long queues of taxpayers
during peak season, ensuring that proper guidelines set forth under LTOM 2nd
Edition 2019 are followed.

3.9 We recommended, and management agreed, to reassess the bond
requirements for AOs and increase the bond amounts to adequately cover their
maximum cash accountabilities, in accordance with the schedule of cash
accountability and bond requirements set by the Bureau of the Treasury.

4.14 We recommended, and the CAO agreed, to identify the details of the
unrecorded reconciling items and make the necessary adjusting entries to correct the
discrepancies in the books.
4.15 Additionally, we reiterated our recommendation, and both the Treasury
Office and the CAO agreed to conduct quarterly reconciliations of their cash records
to identify and promptly address any variances, and to make the corresponding
adjusting entries in their respective books.

5.21 We recommended that the:
a) City Treasurer furnish the CAO with a duly signed, certified list of
taxpayers and their property tax dues at the beginning of each year, and
to furnish an accurate report of tax delinquencies, inclusive of all unpaid
taxes; and
b) CAO and CTO to compare and reconcile their records and work together
proactively to maintain and provide accurate financial records related to
tax receivables
5.22 Also, we recommended, and the CAO agreed, to make adjusting entries to
correct the recording of advance payments by property taxpayers and ensure
compliance with pro-forma accounting entries for recording advance payments.

6.16 We recommended, and the:
a) Local Chief Executive (LCE), with the assistance of the City Accountant,
agreed to consider filing a request for authority to write off dormant
unliquidated cash advances, in compliance with the procedures and guidelines
set forth in COA Circular No. 2016-005; and
b) City Accountant agreed to identify cash advances that are due for liquidation,
issue demand letters if necessary, and prevent the further granting of cash
advances to officers/employees with existing unliquidated cash advances by
regularly monitoring the status of granted cash advances.

7.18 We recommended, and the:
a) LCE agreed to create multiple Inventory Committees to conduct semestral
physical counts of inventories and submit the resulting RPCI to the Audit
Team and the CAO no later than July 31 and January 31 for the first and
second semesters, respectively;
b) CAO and CGSO agreed to enhance current systems or processes to enable
recording of all inventory movements and maintenance of complete SLCs and
SCs;
c) City Accountant agreed to ensure proper accounting treatment for the
purchase of supplies and materials by recognizing inventory items as expenses
only upon consumption or distribution, and reconcile the year-end balances of
inventories based on the submitted RPCI.

8.9 We recommended that the LCE, through the CAO and CGSO, strongly
consider implementing the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances pursuant to
COA Circular No. 2020-006.
8.10 To ensure the success of this initiative, we further recommended and the LCE
agreed to:
a) authorize the creation of Inventory Committees specifically tasked with
completing the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts; and
b) require the members of the said committees to undergo relevant training
to ensure strict adherence to the prescribed guidelines during its
implementation.
8.11 Lastly, we recommended, and the City Accountant agreed, to prepare the
necessary accounting entry to rectify the erroneous recording of an expense as PPE
in the amount of ₱1,951,759.18.

9.9 In view of the foregoing, we reiterated our recommendation, and the City
Accountant agreed to:
a) update and maintain detailed SLs for all CIP accounts, including complete
project information (e.g., project name, location, date started, expected and
actual completion dates, and amount incurred);
b) ensure timely transfer of completed projects to the appropriate PPE or
infrastructure account in compliance with NGAS and COA guidelines; and
c) conduct a reconciliation of SL and GL balances to validate the accuracy and
completeness of reported CIP accounts.

10.37 We recommended, and the following agreed to:
a) CAO: (i) investigate the unreconciled variance of ₱3,442,301.63 between the
GL and SL, and request the write-off of dormant accounts in compliance COA
Circular No. 2023-008; and (ii) adhere to the accrual principle of accounting
in recording CGT’s financial transactions;
b) Management ensure that the terms and conditions set forth in MOA or
equivalent documents are properly followed in the implementation of PPAs
funded by fund transfers from NGAs;
c) BAC submit to the Audit Team the lacking documents noted in the
Construction of TCGH so that this transaction can be further evaluated;
d) CHO continually exercise due diligence in verifying data in the DOH system
to correct any errors in the disbursement of benefits for HCWs and non-
HCWs; and
e) CAO, moving forward, and in light of the insufficient guidelines, record the
20% BFP Fire Code Fee share under the GF rather than the TF. Furthermore,
the utilization of the said fund should undergo the regular appropriation
process and obtain approval from the local Sanggunian.

11.7 We recommended, and the:
a) City Accountant agreed to: (i) adopt a methodology based on historical data
to estimate the portion of leave credits expected to be used or paid within 12
months and classify them as current liabilities, and the remaining accumulated
leave benefits, expected to be payable upon separation, be recognized as long-
term liabilities in compliance with IPSAS 39; and (ii) recognize leave benefits
yearly as they are earned, rather than recording the entire liability in a single
year;
b) City Budget Officer agreed to establish an Employee Benefits Reserve Fund
on a yearly basis to ensure sufficient cash reserves for future monetization; and
c) Human Resource Department agreed to coordinate with the CAO and City
Budget Office (CBO) to ensure adherence to Rule XVI of the Omnibus Rules
Implementing Book V of EO No. 292 on the yearly accounting and funding of
leave benefits.

12.7 We recommended, and the:
a) City Accountant agreed to: (i) reverse the unauthorized write-off or
derecognition of accounts by making the necessary adjusting entries; and (ii)
ensure that COA Circular No. 2023-008 guidelines on the proper disposition
of dormant accounts are strictly followed before any future write-offs or
derecognitions;
b) LCE agreed to authorize the creation of a committee: (i) to determine the
cause(s) of the unavailability and/or loss of records; and (ii) take the necessary
steps prescribed under COA Circular No. 2023-008 to avail of the one-time
cleansing of dormant accounts.

13.16 We recommended, and the City LSB agreed to:
a) Ensure compliance with DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, Series of
2017 by submitting copies of the SEF budget for CY 2024 to the City Budget
Officer, City Accountant, and City Treasurer within seven (7) days after
approval;
b) Submit the required SEF utilization reports to the Sanggunian Panlungsod
(SP) ng Tarlac and the Regional Offices of the DBM and DILG, in addition to
DepEd CO, as mandated;
c) Prepare and submit the annual SEF utilization report to comply with
reporting requirements;
d) Clarify and document the roles and responsibilities of LSB members, ensuring
that the Board Secretariats is fully accountable for the preparation,
submission, and safekeeping of SEF reports.
13.17 We also recommend, and the BAC agreed to:
a) Submit all lacking procurement documentation mentioned in paragraph 15.10
so that these transactions that have not yet undergone audit may be evaluated
further; and
b) Strengthen internal controls by implementing a systematic review process for
(DVs) to ensure all transactions have complete and proper documentation
before approval.

14.34 We recommended, and the:
a) CEO agreed to: (i) require contractors to repair the identified defects within
the warranty period, in accordance with the contract terms and conditions;
(ii) strengthen monitoring and supervision during project implementation by
requiring the CEO and concerned personnel to conduct regular and
systematic inspections, especially at critical stages of construction; and (iii)
ensure compliance with safety standards by requiring all workers to wear
proper PPE at project sites and by posting the mandated tarpaulin project
signage to promote transparency and public awareness;
b) Local Finance Committee agreed to: (i) revisit the current practice of
allocating the 2% EAO from the 20% DF and assess its alignment with JMC
No. 1 guidelines; and (ii) seek alternative funding sources for administrative
and personnel-related expenditures;
c) CAO agreed to make adjusting entries to record the EAO expenses by
reversing the incorrect capitalization of EAO expenses in the PPE accounts
and properly recording salaries and office supplies as operational expenses;
d) BAC agreed to: (i) submit the lacking procurement documentation noted
above so that these transactions may be subjected to further audit evaluation;
(ii) strengthen internal controls by implementing a systematic review process
for DVs to ensure all transactions have complete and proper documentation
before approval; (iii) require that all uncompleted and ongoing portions of
projects be fully considered in the computation of the NFCC as provided in
Section 23.4.1.4 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184;
14.35 Also, we reiterated our prior audit recommendations, and management
agreed to:
a) indicate clear and specific implementation timelines in both the LDIP and AIP
to support timely execution and completion of development projects; and
b) complete the submission of outstanding supporting documents related to CY
2023 projects, as listed in Annex C.
14.36 Lastly, we reiterated that the management:
a) update the CDP and LDIP to include specific PPAs aligned with the 20% DF,
essuring their proper inclusion in the AIP; and
b) facilitate the submission of advance copies of contracts and their supporting
documents allow timely audit review.

15.21 We recommended, and the:
a) Local Finance Committee agreed to: (i) ensure that LDRRMF funds are used
strictly for allowable expenses, as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-
002, to prevent misuse or misallocation of disaster risk reduction funds; and
(ii) review the 2% allocation for EAO and ensure that funds are drawn from
appropriate sources, without compromising the integrity of LGU funds, which
have a mandated list of allowable expenses;
b) CAO agreed to make adjusting entries to record the EAO expenses by
reversing the incorrect capitalization of EAO expenses in the Property, Plant,
and Equipment (PPE) accounts and properly recording salaries and office
supplies as operational expenses;
c) CBO, CTO, and CAO agreed to: (i) strictly adhere to the principle of separate
fund accounting, particularly in the charging and accounting for
disbursements chargeable against the STF for LDRRMF; and
(ii) ensure that all future STF-funded obligations are processed under the
appropriate fund to avoid financial misstatements
d) BAC agreed to: (i) submit all lacking procurement documentation so that
these transactions may be subjected to further audit evaluation; and (ii)
strengthen internal controls by implementing a systematic review process for
DVs to ensure all transactions have complete and proper documentation
before approval.

16.8 We recommended, and the Tarlac City GFPS, agreed to: a) continue to be
capacitated on GAD, particularly on the use of gender analysis/assessment tools such
as GMEF, GeRL Assessment Tool, HGDG, GFAsT; and b) incorporate in the
succeeding GPBs the results of gender analysis/assessment using the prescribed GAD
tools.

17.34 We recommended, and the:
City Health Office (CHO), CGSO and Bids and Awards Committee (BAC):
a) Provide a written explanation and/or supporting justification for the
procurement of drugs not listed in the current PNF-EML, in accordance with
90
the provisions of DOH Administrative Order No. 2012-003;
b) Conduct an investigation into the excess unit prices noted in paragraph 1.12,
and determine the circumstances that led to these discrepancies. The results of
the investigation shall be submitted to the Audit Team for further evaluation
and determination of appropriate audit actions;
c) Ensure that all procured pharmaceutical products comply with the technical
specifications and compositions prescribed in the PNF-EML, which is
regularly updated and serves as the official reference for government drug
procurement, unless a valid exemption has been duly granted;
d) Strictly adhere to the DPRI during the procurement of drugs and medicines,
and incorporate a price validation step in the procurement process, whereby
responsible officers shall review and verify all quoted prices against the latest
DPRI;
BAC and CAO to:
e) Submit all the noted lacking procurement documentation as required under
Paragraph 9.1.3.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012, so that
these transactions can be further evaluated; and
CAO to:
f) (i) strictly comply with Sections 51 and 114 of the Manual on the NGAS for
LGUs, Volume I, by recording purchases of drugs and medicines under the
appropriate Inventory accounts using the perpetual inventory method, and only
recognizing these items as expenses upon issuance or consumption; and (ii)
establish or strengthen inventory monitoring controls, ensure the maintenance of
detailed inventory records, and conduct regular reconciliation between the
General Ledger and the inventory subsidiary records to promote transparency,
safeguard inventory assets, and ensure accurate financial reporting.

18.29 We recommended, and the:
a) BAC agreed to: (i) update its procurement documents to include specific,
measurable, and detailed technical specifications for each item in accordance
with Section 17.2 of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184; and (ii)) consider
consolidating the procurement of similar items (e.g., school kits) into a single
public bidding process when feasible, to maximize efficiency, minimize
administrative costs, and achieve economies of scale;
b) CGSO or responsible unit agreed to: (i) enhance internal review procedures
for POs before issuance to ensure that all mandatory information is included;
and (ii) ensure that IARs are properly and completely accomplished
including a comprehensive descriptions and inspection findings before acceptance and
approval; and
c) BAC and CAO agreed to: (i) implement a complete documentation checklist
before awarding any contract or processing payments, to guarantee full
compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-001 and R.A. No. 9184
documentation requirements; and (ii) submit all missing procurement
documentation mentioned in paragraph 21.19, so that these transactions,
which have not yet undergone audit, can be further evaluated.

19.22 We recommended, and the:
a) City Treasurer agreed to: (i) establish procedures to ascertain the accuracy of
business tax assessments and collections by identifying and prioritizing
businesses for examination of their books of accounts, particularly those with
significant discrepancies; and (ii) pursue the enforcement and collection of
identified business tax deficiencies to ensure recovery of under-collected
revenues; and (iii) consider cross-checking declarations with in-house data
such as CAO Check Disbursement Journal and/or third-party data (e.g., BIR
filings, audited financial statements);
b) BPLO-Head agreed to: (i) ensure the completeness of documentary
requirements prior to the issuance of business permits, ensuring that all listed
documents, unless officially exempted, are on file, and documenting
exemptions clearly, (ii) or consider the recommending the formal revision of
the Citizen’s Charter, to include specific exemption clauses for micro or small
businesses and vulnerable sectors (e.g., market vendors, small-scale traders),
thereby aligning actual practice with published policy and eliminating informal
discretionary decisions.

20.24 We recommended, and the:
a) CSWDO and Mayor’s Office agreed to: (i) expedite the completion and formal
adoption of internal guidelines tailored to the City’s specific context, in order
to address procedural gaps, standardize documentary requirements, and
ensure the consistent implementation of assistance programs; (ii) clearly
define acceptable forms of proof (e.g., barangay-issued certifications with
official dry seals, valid government-issued IDs), and establish procedures for
verifying the authenticity of submitted documents directly with barangay
offices; (iii) establish a clear delineation of responsibilities between the
Mayor’s Office and the CSWDO in processing financial assistance to avoid
overlap or ambiguity in roles; (iii) consider developing and maintaining a
centralized digital registry of all financial and burial assistance granted, to
help identify potential duplicate or inconsistent claims and serve as a reliable
reference for future validations; and
b) BAC and CAO agreed to submit the lacking procurement documentation
noted above so that these transactions may be subjected to further audit
evaluation.

21.5 We recommended and the CHRMO, in coordination with the CAO, agreed to
ensure the accuracy of amounts deducted from employees and remitted to the GSIS,
HDMF, and PHIC. Specifically, the following actions are recommended and
Management agreed to: (a) implement a more effective monitoring system to ensure
the accuracy of contributions, loan amortizations, and remittances; b) facilitate
regular reconciliation of records to promptly identify and address discrepancies
between the respective records; (c) review and validate carry-over balances from
prior years and secure supporting documentation, or initiate proper clearing
procedures.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to instruct the:
i) PCF Custodian, through the City Treasurer, to fully liquidate the Petty Cash every end of the year and inform all offices concerned that any expenses not entered in the PCF during the year will be recorded as Accounts Payable;
ii) Treasury and Accounting Offices to reconcile their cash records quarterly to identify any variances and make adjusting entries in their respective books if needed; and
iii) Accounting Office to make adjusting entries on the erroneous recording of 32 checks and identify the nature of the unrecorded bank reconciling items for proper recording in their respective accounts.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to instruct the:
PCF Custodian, through the City Treasurer, to fully liquidate the Petty Cash every end of the year and inform all offices concerned that any expenses not entered in the PCF during the year will be recorded as Accounts Payable;
Treasury and Accounting Offices to reconcile their cash records quarterly to identify any variances and make adjusting entries in their respective books if needed; and
Accounting Office to make adjusting entries on the erroneous recording of 32 checks and identify the nature of the unrecorded bank reconciling items for proper recording in their respective accounts.

We reiterated and the LCE agreed to instruct the City Accountant to ensure proper accounting treatment for purchases of supplies and materials by recognizing inventory items as expenses only upon consumption/distribution.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the City Accountant to: i) update the subsidiary records of the CIP accounts; ii) adjust the PPE accounts to recognize completed projects and ensure that the CIP accounts reflect only the accumulated costs of partially completed infrastructure projects; and iii) record adjusting entry for depreciation that had accumulated, counting from the date of completion of PPE accounts.
Also, we recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the CEO to submit a quarterly status report of infrastructure projects to the CAO for reconciliation purposes.
The Accounting Office commented that reconciliation procedures of CIP accounts for CY 2019 to the present have already started.

We reiterated that the LCE a) require the CAO and the CGSO to i) maintain close coordination with each other so as to immediately determine the causes of the noted discrepancies between their respective records; ii) make certain that necessary procedures and adjustments are promptly taken up whenever the said causes are identified; and iii) consider the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006.
We further recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the CAO to exert efforts in retrieving prior year’s data to be able to recognize depreciation expenses for all depreciable PPEs.
The Accounting Office, together with CGSO, commented that they are planning to avail the seminar to be conducted by COA for the one-time cleansing of PPE pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006 to establish the accuracy and reliability of the recorded balance.
We recommended, and the LCE agreed, that the CAO strictly adhere to the principle of separate fund accounting, particularly in accounting for disbursements chargeable against the STF for LDRRMF.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the CPDO and all Department Heads involved in the planning process to: i) be specific in including PPAs in the CDP and LDIP to ensure its inclusion in the AIP for the constituents to enjoy benefits to be derived therefrom; and ii) indicate the specific date of implementation of PPAs in the LDIP and AIP to ensure the timely delivery of the project to the intended users/beneficiaries.
Lastly, we recommended, and the Management agreed, to continually maximize the utilization of the 20% DF for the continuous development of the City, which will eventually uplift the lives of the constituents.
The CPDO and CEO commented that they will try to amend the CDP and LDIP to include the observations noted by the Audit Team. However, it may take time for their full implementation.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the a) CDRRMO to submit the Revised LDRRM AIP as approved by the local sanggunian and LCE upon recommendation of the LDRRMC in accordance with the approved LDRRMP; b) City Accountant to i) identify and determine the costs and accumulated depreciation of all equipment (excluding heavy equipment) used for response and rescue activities and subsequently prepare the necessary accounting entries to reclassify the identified equipment to the Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment account; and ii) coordinate with concerned offices to facilitate the submission of the documents enumerated under paragraphs 13.15 and 13.16.

We reiterated that the LCE instruct the City Accountant, in coordination with Heads or Officer-in-Charge (OIC) of the fund transfer from NGAs, to: i) exert effort to reconcile the noted difference between the GL and SL amounting to ₱3,442,306.63; and ii) gather supporting documents for the three projects totaling ₱4,078,803.18 to facilitate the liquidation and/or write off.
Also, we reiterated and the LCE agreed to instruct the OIC of the fund transfer from NGAs to: i) strictly observe the timeframe of the implementation of the projects to ensure efficient delivery of public service to the intended beneficiaries to avoid the recurrence of similar lapses in the future; and ii) ensure the submission to the Source Agency concerned of liquidation documents of funds received from NGAs.
Lastly, we recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the Local Finance Committee (LFC) to post the reports on fund utilization and status of the program/project of LGSF-funded projects on three conspicuous places and in the LGU’s website.
The Accounting Office replied that they have tried to reconcile the Due to NGAs account and looked for relevant supporting documents, but were still unable to locate the needed documents/files. Thus, implementation for the unreconciled amount may take some time.

We recommended and the Accounting Office agreed to: i) adjust the balances of Due to NGAs accounts to reflect the correct amount based on the certification or confirmation provided by the source agencies; ii) provide the Audit Team with a copy of Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) showing the adjusting journal entries; and iii) regularly coordinate with source agencies for the purpose of reconciling records.
The Accounting Office, as per their reply to the Audit Team, has already recorded adjusting entries for the account Due to NGAS under the TF.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the (i) BAC Chairman and BAC Secretariat, in coordination with the City Budget Officer, to submit the City’s approved APP and Supplemental APP to the GPPB and COA; and strictly observe the regulations prescribed by the revised IRR of RA No. 9184; (ii) BAC Chairman and BAC Secretariat to invite Auditors and other observers, in writing, in all stages of procurement process including the bid evaluation and post-qualification proceedings; (iii) BAC Secretariat and  concerned departments to submit an advance copy of contract and supporting documents to the Audit Team; (iv) BAC and BAC Secretariat to submit explanation/justification on deficiency noted on the advance bidding process conducted for the Fabrication of Concrete Barriers; and (v) TWG, BAC Secretariat, and concerned departments to submit the lacking documentary requirements to the Audit Team and/or provide justification if the lacking document/s is/are not applicable to the project.
Management has already submitted the lacking documents as stated in item 16.28 and are subject for validation by the Audit Team.

We recommended and LCE agreed to i) make certain that the power and authority to assess business taxes is exclusively exercised by the City Treasurer as mandated by Section 171 of R.A. No. 7160; and ii) require the City Accountant, in coordination with the City Treasurer, to implement the business tax withholding policy on future money payments to suppliers with business offices in Tarlac City in accordance with the Revised Revenue Code of Tarlac City.
Also, we recommended and the City Treasurer agreed to make use of the best available data to determine which businesses to subject to the examination of books of accounts in order to ascertain the correctness of assessment and collection of business tax.
During the Exit Conference, the Management informed that they will be formulating the revised procedures for the application of Business Permit to include the Audit Team’s observation.

We reiterated and the LCE, as the Chairperson of the GFPS, agreed to ensure that i) gender analysis to determine the gender-responsiveness of regular PPAs be conducted prior to their attribution in the GPB; and ii) during the preparation of the GAD AR, the same PPAs be subjected to the HGDG test using the PIMME checklist to determine the actual cost of expenditures that can be attributed to the GAD budget.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Disbursing Officers to ensure separation of money for each cash advance to eliminate the risk of cash mixed-up and precise placement of signatures/thumbmarks of beneficiaries in the payroll.

We reiterated our previous recommendations and the LCE agreed to instruct the CGSO to come up with the Local Road Inventory and Road Map and Local Road Network Property Card for all roads under the CGT’s jurisdiction.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to instruct the
City Accountant to ensure that all purchases of supplies and materials are recorded
in the proper inventory account and recognized as expense upon consumption only.

We reiterated and the AOs agreed to ensure the i) separation of money for each
cash advance to eliminate the risk of commingling of cash funds; and ii) precise
placement of signatures/thumbmarks of beneficiaries in the payroll.

Also, we recommended and the CTO agreed to formulate a proper disbursing
procedure for the yearly Senior Citizen’s Benefit payout.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the i) City Treasurer to
furnish the CAO a certified list of taxpayers with the amount due and collectible for
the year pursuant to Section 20 of the MNGAS for LGUs; and ii) City Accountant to
a) prepare the necessary adjusting/correcting entries on the errors made in the
recording of the collections of advance RPT and SET payments; b) use the Prior
Years’ Adjustments account in recording the adjustment/s of prior years’
transactions affecting revenue and expenses and other adjustments which increase or
decrease the Retained Operating Surplus account; and c) ensure that accounting
procedures being implemented are in line with the provisions of IPSAS 23 and
MNGAS for LGUs with regard to the recording of RPT transactions.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the CAO and the CGSO
to: a) maintain complete PPELCs and PCs, respectively, for all properties; b)
continue exerting diligent efforts in the reconciliation of all PPE accounts; and c)
consider the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts balance pursuant to COA Circular
No. 2020-006. We further recommended and the CAO agreed to prepare the
necessary accounting entries to record the transfer of completed projects from the TF
to the GF.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the City Accountant to i) conduct a
thorough verification of all the outstanding payables to ensure that any obligation
which had been outstanding for two years or more and without actual claim or which
were not covered by perfected contracts on record are reverted to the unappropriated
surplus; and ii) require the completion of accounts payable aging schedule under the
SEF.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the City Accountant to
ensure proper classification of Loans Payable in the Statement of Financial Position
by presenting the portion due to be settled within 12 months after the reporting date
as Current Liabilities and the remaining portion as Non-Current Liabilities.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the i) CDRRMO to determine the use
of the previous years’ unexpended LDRRMF balances and incorporate the identified PPAs in the LDRRMFIP; ii) concerned officers/employees to make certain that the
requirements enumerated in Paragraph 12.14 are complied with; and iii) City Accountant
to ensure a) that unexpended balances of LDRRMF exceeding the five-year utilization
period are reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the GF; and b) the completeness of
documents before endorsing DVs for payment pursuant to Section 4 (6) of P.D. No. 1445,
COA Circular No. 2012-001, and the related provisions of the Revised IRR of R.A. No.
9184.

We recommended and Management agreed that:
a) CAO exert effort to reconcile the noted difference between the GL and SL
amounting ₱3,442,306.63; and
b) City Health Office (CHO) explain the deficiency noted on the two duplicate
names in the payroll.

Also, we recommended and the LCE agreed to require the City Accountant, in
coordination with other Heads or Officers In-Charge of the funds received from
NGAs, to:
a) ensure the submission to the Audit Team of a copy of the MOA or its
equivalent on the fund transfers received from NGAs;
b) monitor the submission to the Source Agency concerned of liquidation
documents of funds received from NGAs; and
c) strictly observe the timeframe of implementation schedules of the projects to
ensure efficient delivery of public service to the intended beneficiaries to avoid
the recurrence of similar lapses in the future.

We reiterated and the LCE agreed to : i) require the GFPS to a) submit the
GPB and GAD AR to the Audit Team within the periods prescribed in COA Circular
No. 2014-001; and b) administer the HGDG and PIMME tools to measure the actual
cost attributed to gender-responsiveness; ii) institutionalize the Tarlac City GFPS; and
iii) ensure that all GFPS members and concerned officials/personnel undergo
capacity building programs on GAD.

We recommended and
Management agreed to instruct (i) the Bids and Award Committee (BAC) Secretariat to submit the requisite documents mentioned in paragraph 5.3(a) to assist the Audit Team in rendering conclusion on the legality of the award of the contracts to the concerned suppliers; (ii) the City Treasurer to immediately cause the automated fund transfer from the GF to TF of the ₱2.5 million donations received from TCWD; and (iii) the CGSO to expedite the submission of DV No. 100-21-07-1295 to the Accounting Office and thereafter to the Audit Team to facilitate the conduct of post audit of the procurement transaction.

We reiterated our previous recommendations and Management agreed that (a) the City Accountant (i) issue demand letters to all concerned AOs for them to liquidate their cash advances by submitting complete and valid settlement documents or refunding applicable portion of the cash advance, as necessary; and (ii) thoroughly monitor cash advances granted to different AOs by making a schedule when these cash advances are due for liquidation and issue demand letters, if needed; and (b) through the CGT Officials, consider applying for write-off of unliquidated cash advances aged more than 10 years and secure authority from the COA to derecognize the accounts after approval thereof, taking into account the procedures and guidelines prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We reiterated our previous recommendations and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to:
(a) Formalize the creation of the CGT’s Inventory Committee to spearhead the conduct of annual physical count of PPE and require the same to submit accordingly the RPCPPE to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year pursuant to Section 124 of Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I;
b) Require the CGSO and CAO to (i) maintain PCs and PPELCs, respectively, for all LGU properties and reconcile the noted discrepancies while taking into account the necessary adjustment in the books, as necessary; (ii) collate all Deeds of Donation for donated assets to facilitate the derecognition of assets in the books of accounts; and
c) Strongly consider the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the (a) concerned officials to ensure the optimal utilization of the 20% DF; (b) Budget Office to coordinate with Accounting Office regarding the actual amount to be obligated for each project of 20% DF and perform reconciliation regularly; (c) BAC Secretariat to submit to the Audit Team the posting to PhilGEPS of the NOA, NTP and Contract; and (d) BAC Secretariat/concerned officials to submit the approved contracts within five (5) days from execution thereof in consonance with COA Circular 2009-01.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the:
a) CDRRMO to (i) observe proper monitoring of LDRRMF PPAs to ensure prompt and timely implementation of the same; and (ii) warrant the optimal utilization of the LDRRMF, particularly the 70% to achieve the objective of reducing disaster risk and enhancing disaster management capabilities;
b) Concerned officials to submit the requisite documents enumerated in paragraphs 11.9 and 11.10 to the Audit Team and observe compliance with the provisions of GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 in the emergency procurement of goods; and
c) BAC and Technical Working Group (TWG) to thoroughly examine and evaluate bidding documents in strict compliance with the provisions of R.A. No. 9184 and its revised IRR.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct (a) all concerned City officials and personnel (i) to strictly adhere to the provisions concerning the implementation of projects funded by LGSF; and (ii) to submit the requisite documents to the Audit Team for evaluation and examination and observe stringent compliance on the submission of documentary requirements of transactions; (b) the City Accountant to review thoroughly all claims presented for payment prior to approval to ensure that only properly and completely documented transactions/disbursement are paid; and (c) the City Treasurer and City Accountant to immediately return to the National Treasury all unutilized amounts from the LGSF as of December 31, 2021 and record the same in the books of accounts.

in formulating the GPB. We also recommended that the GFPS submit GAD AR to
the Audit Team within the period prescribed in COA Circular No. 2014-001.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) i) enjoin the responsible officials of the agency to submit to the Audit Team the requisite documents enumerated in paragraphs 5.17 and 5.18; and ii) direct the Acting Provincial Accountant to ensure that supporting documents are complete before payment is made to prove the regularity of claims.

We reiterated to recommend that the (a) Acting Provincial Accountant and Acting Provincial Treasurer regularly reconcile their records for the timely settlement of the discrepancies between the Cash-in-Bank account balance as recorded per GL and CB; and (b) Acting Provincial Accountant (i) cause the immediate recording of book reconciling items identified in the BRS to present the accurate financial position of the Agency; (ii) request records from the Treasury Office to effect closing in the GL the 24 bank accounts that are no longer existing; and (iii) take up necessary adjustments to revert the cash to the treasury for the identified stale checks.

We reiterated our PYs’ recommendations that Management i) locate the whereabouts of those AOs who are still alive, for them to settle their balances; and ii) exert more effort in securing the necessary supporting documents cited under COA Circular 2016-05 dated December 19, 2016 to be able to apply for request for write-off of the dormant accounts in order to present the correct financial position of the Agency.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Provincial Accountant to i) reconcile the reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds in GF and Due to Other Funds in TF; ii) make the necessary adjusting entries to correct the omissions on recording liquidations and fund transfers; and iii) ensure the timely recording and adjustments thereof.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Acting Provincial Accountant to i) send confirmation letters to concerned agencies to determine existence of documents to support the claim and to ascertain whether counterpart entries were recorded in their respective book of accounts; ii) provide adequate provision for impairment loss for the long outstanding receivables in accordance with IPSAS 26; and  iii) prepare the necessary documents prescribed in the COA Circular to support the request for authority to write off dormant receivables, once obtained from the concerned agencies.

We recommended that the (a) PSWDO and PGSO furnish the Accounting Office copy of distribution list for inventories held for distribution and prepare the SAR thereon; (b) Acting Provincial Accountant to (i) continuously correct the balances of account “Other Payables” in the TF book by recognizing the proper drugs, medicines and related inventory accounts in recording procurement made in prior years; and (ii) make the necessary adjustments to reflect the omission and correct the errors in posting supplies and materials consumption; (c) GSO assign each office’s  Supply Officer in charge  to  receive, prepare and submit their respective monthly supply consumption report; (d) PGSO and Inventory Committee devote time to conduct the periodic physical count of inventory and render reports thereon.

We recommended that the (a) Acting Provincial Accountant and the Acting Provincial General Services Officer exert efforts (i) through the Inventory Committee, perform annual physical count of all classes of PPE owned by and/or titled to the PGT in order to continue reconciling the discrepancies of the records between the two offices and conform with the guidelines and procedures on inventory taking, recognition of those found at station and disposition for non-existing/missing PPE items for the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2020-006; (ii) coordinate and record all donated PPE items during the year including unrecorded PPE items from previous years, if there are any; (iii) prepare and maintain the required records and reports of all PPE accounts; and (iv) facilitate the reclassification of properties falling under Investment Property in accordance with IPSAS 16; and  (b) the Acting Provincial Accountant instruct the concerned personnel (i) to compute depreciation expense and accumulated depreciation for all depreciable assets; and (ii) continue to trace the records of old PPE accounts which are still under the TF and transfer the same once their records have been verified.

We reiterated to recommend that Management direct the Accounting Department to i) exert effort to determine sufficient details of the Due from NGAs and GOCCs accounts in order to regularly monitor their use and ensure the implementation of the projects/programs for which they were granted; ii) perform periodic reconciliation of  the unliquidated fund transfers by sending confirmation letters, coordinating with the source agencies and returning the unutilized balances; and iii) file a request for authority to write-off dormant fund transfers to the COA after the conditions and requirements for which have been fully satisfied and complied with.

We recommended that the LCE (a) require the LDRRM Council to allocate the current appropriations fund of LDRRMF to 30% QRF and 70% MF in accordance with Section 21 of R.A. No. 10121; (b) direct the Acting Provincial Accountant to (i) follow the perpetual inventory method in recording of supplies as stated in Section 114 of NGAS Manual; (ii) prepare the necessary adjusting entries on the erroneous recording to “Medical, Dental and Laboratory Supplies for distribution” account of medical supplies for consumption; (iii) continue to transfer the unspent current year’s LDRRMF to STF account and revert back to the GF the unexpended balance the STF after five (5) years; and (c) enjoin the concerned officials of the agency to submit the deficient documents to the Audit Team and ensure the completeness of documents before payment to substantiate the regularity of claims.

We recommended that the LCE i) direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) members and the Acting Provincial Engineer to observe the timeliness in the procurement planning as well as in the conduct of all stages of the procurement process to accelerate project implementation; and ii) instruct the Acting Provincial Planning and Development Officer (PPDO) and the Acting Provincial Engineer to conduct close supervision and monitoring of the implementation of infrastructure projects so that proper interventions may be applied in order to fast track their completion within the stipulated period.

We recommended that Management i) continue to maximize the utilization of its SEF in order to deliver its services to the intended beneficiaries; and ii) instruct the custodian of the above-mentioned items to submit the required distribution lists for the manuals, hygiene kits and tablets for validation of the Audit Team.

We reiterated to recommend that the LCE, being the Chairman of the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS), to i) instruct the GFPS personnel through the PPDO to update the GAD database which has remained the same for the last four years; and ii) further instruct personnel in charge to fully implement the use of the HGDG assessment tool and attribute only a portion of the agency’s major programs to the GAD budget to reflect the correct amount pertaining to GAD accomplishment.

We recommended that the PSWMB, through PENRO, (i) continue to provide assistance to the remaining component LGU in the preparation of its SWMP in accordance with the requirement of the law to finally secure the approval of NSWMC in CY 2022; (ii) continue to extend assistance to the component LGUs in updating their SWMP; and (iii) continue to implement the Solid Waste Management and Health Care Management program and activities aligned with the provisions of R.A. No. 9003 to promote environmental protection and public health.

We recommended that Management require the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) to i) strictly enforce and prioritize deduction and remittance of loan amortizations for GSIS loans; and ii) coordinate with the GSIS Office to update and reconcile their records and identify the personnel concerned so that restructuring or immediate payments can be made on their due and demandable loans.

We recommended that the OIC-Provincial Accountant i) continue to comply with BIR RMO No. 23-2014 on the withholding and remittance of taxes to the BIR; and ii) require the concerned accounting personnel to review the accounting records and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to record the omitted transaction as mentioned above.

We commended Management for the efficient collection performance and recommended that the LCE i) require the Local Finance Committee (LFC) to identify additional sources of income, if not the passage of an updated Revenue Code; and ii) instruct the Acting Provincial Treasurer to intensify collection efforts on the sources of income which registered shortfall from the estimated targets.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor continue to instruct the Acting Provincial Treasurer and the OIC-Provincial Accountant to i) monitor and cause the immediate settlement of the balances of audit suspensions by persons responsible either through cash payments or submission of the lacking information or documents within the prescribed period; and ii) require persons liable named in the audit disallowances to settle their accounts through cash payments, unless they are awaiting COA decisions on their appeal.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed that (a) the City Accountant (i) issue demand letters to all concerned accountable officers for them liquidate their cash advances by submitting complete and valid settlement documents or refunding applicable portion of the cash advance, as necessary; and (ii) establish a thorough monitoring of cash advances granted to different Accountable Officers by making a schedule when these cash advances are due for liquidation and issue demand letters, if needed; and (b) through the CGT Officials, consider applying for write-off of unliquidated cash advances aged more than ten years and secure authority from the COA to derecognize the accounts after approval thereof, taking into account the procedures and guidelines prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the (a) City Treasurer furnish the CAO with the certification of collection target for the year together with the duly certified list of taxpayers at the beginning of the year; and (b) City Accountant (i) rectify the balances of SET Receivable and Deferred SET Income accounts by preparing an adjusting entry on the noted discrepancy of ₱902,097.22 between the two accounts; and (ii) review the accounting procedures with respect to recording of RPT transactions and faithfully observe the provisions of Section 20 of Manual on the NGAS for LGU, Volume I thereon.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the City Accountant (a)  exhaust all remedies to gather the supporting documents needed for the receivables noted; (b) henceforth, consider the write-off of dormant receivables for more than ten years in the books of accounts, in accordance with the procedures set forth by COA Circular No. 2016- 005; (c) facilitate the liquidation of Advances for Operating Expenses; (d) continue to trace the erroneous posting in the Advances for Operating Expenses account and effect any adjustments, as necessary; (e) recognize only the final and executory audit disallowances and charges in the account Receivables – Disallowances/Charges; (f) update the records on the account Receivables – Disallowances/Charges and reconcile with the Audit Team’s records in order to make the necessary adjustment in the books; (g) submit to the Audit Team the documents related to the settlement of Mr. Ricky Tabag to facilitate the issuance of Notice of Settlement of Suspension/Disallowance/Charge; (h) continue identifying the reconciling items between these reciprocal accounts and effect the adjustments in the accounting records, as necessary; and (i) institute a thorough monitoring of these reciprocal accounts by ensuring that entries made/posted from one account has a parallel recognition/posting to the other.

We recommended and the LCE concurred, thru the City Accountant to (a) make the necessary corrections/adjustments in the inventory and expense accounts affected; (b) observe the perpetual method in recognizing inventory and avoid recording outright expense when accounting for drugs and medicines: and (c) observe the proper recording of inventory items and its relative expense account as they take place in accordance with IPSAS 12 and Section 114 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended and the LCE is amenable that the City Accountant derecognize the cost of the procured inventories recorded under Other Machinery and Equipment account amounting to ₱8,500,000.00 and accordingly, make adjustments on the expense/Government Equity accounts upon issuance thereof.

We recommended and the LCE concurred (a) to continue upholding the CGT’s mandate in promoting local development by appropriating 20% DF of its annual Internal Revenue Allotment (IRA) pursuant to the provisions of the LGC and DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1; (b) instruct the (i) City Engineer and City Planning Chief ensure the delivery in CY 2021 of the remaining three projects programmed in CY 2020; (ii) City Budget Chief take-up obligations based on ALOBS (or counterpart Certification of Appropriations, Funds and Obligation of Allotment (CAFOA) as prescribed in the Government Accounting Manual (GAM) for LGUs, Volume I, beginning CY 2021) instead of the amount of appropriation; and (iii) the City Budget Chief and City Accountant perform monthly reconciliation of records to reflect the accurate balances of obligations at a given period.

We recommended and the LCE is amenable that the (a) BAC and concerned officials in charge of procurement to continue to use the Checklist of Requirements and align its contents to the processes and documents established in RA No. 9184, infusing the observations and recommendations of the Audit Team; (b) BAC together with the TWG (i) prepare post-qualification evaluation reports; (ii) require the submission of security bond by the LCB before contract execution; (iii) post the NTP, NOA and Contract of Awards in the PhilGEPS in compliance with RA No. 9184; and (iv) submit the lacking documents as enumerated in the discussion; (c) City Engineer (i) demand from the contractors Warranty Security before issuance of Certificate of Final Acceptance; and (ii) ensure that project contractors observe the proper details to be provided on project signboards including the update thereof to reflect the actual project status; and (d) City Accountant check and ensure the completeness of documents before effecting payment.

We recommended and the LCE agreed (a) that the TCSWMB-TWG (i) prepare an Annual Work Plan and Budget showing the SWM activities to be implemented and the financial counterpart for each SWM activity, taking into consideration the initiatives identified in the Ten-year SWM Plan; and (ii) organize monitoring and evaluation team and require them to prepare Progress Reports in line with the Annual Work Plan and the SWM Plan; (b) to (i) consider reinforcing the CENRO to carve out more progress in the implementation of SWMP; and (ii) require the cooperation of the Barangays in the implementation of  SWM particularly the establishment of operational MRF and campaign for proper waste segregation; and (c) that the CENRO Chief and the City Planning Chief design contingency plans in order to put the City back on track in implementing the Ten-Year SWM Plan.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) reconstitute and/or designate/fill-up the members of the ExeCom and TWG of the Tarlac City GFPS; (b) capacitate the GFPS and concerned officials with the mechanisms and processes in mainstreaming GAD perspective in the LGU through trainings, conferences, symposia administered by the PCW, DILG or other NGAs directly involved in GAD concerns; (c) provide the concerned GFPS personnel with equipment such as laptops/computers and other necessary devices for the monitoring of GAD-related accomplishments; and (d) instruct the GFPS to (i) equip themselves with the HGDG tool and PIMME to determine how much of the CGT’s actual disbursements for implementing its major programs can be attributed to GAD; and (ii) enhance the AR for GAD by completely filling-out the needed qualitative aspect of the activities implemented including data on sex-aggregation pursuant to MCW, as necessary.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the (a) City Accountant (i) ensure that all disbursements are supported with complete documentation and that necessary data/information are provided before payment; (ii) submit the lacking documents as enumerated in the preceding observation; and (iii) secure from the DepEd Accountant a certification confirming that all expenses chargeable against the SEF are net of the budgetary provision for the same or related item(s) in the DepEd budget; (b) LSB (i) carefully identify the needed projects that would create bigger impact such as prioritizing infrastructure projects like construction and repair of school buildings; and (ii) in cooperation with the City Engineer strive to fully utilize the SEF in accordance with the priority programs/projects to realize their foremost value for the benefit of stakeholders in the education sector; and (c) BAC Secretariat verify the signature of the representatives of suppliers to ensure that the City is dealing only with the authorized suppliers’ representatives.

We recommended and the LCE concurred that the AAO (a) continue the effort to expedite the settlement of the remaining past due accounts of concerned CGT employees with the GSIS to fulfill the 90%-95% settlement commitment in the early part of CY 2021; (b) give preference to settlement of GSIS loans over bank and other private loans in compliance with RA No. 11465; and (c) deduct loan amortization from the payroll of concerned employees on a monthly basis and remit the same on or before the tenth day of the month following, in accordance with RA No. 8291.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the (a) City Planning Chief and City Engineer to continue their collaborative efforts in pursuing the remaining and future LGSF-funded programs in accordance with the wisdom of LGSF program of DILG; (b) BAC and its Secretariat submit to the Audit Team the lacking documents as enumerated herein and warrant the completeness of supporting documents that need to be prepared/submitted in the procurement stages of projects in accordance with the provisions of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR; and (c) City Accountant ensure that all necessary documents to be attached to the DVs are present before making the payment/s.
We recommended and the LCE agreed to continue to implement meaningful projects that uplift the condition of life of the constituents of the CGT.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) assign personnel who will be responsible in the receipt, inspection, acceptance, recording, systems encoding, and issuance of medicines. 

We also recommended and the LCE concurred to direct the (a) Pharmacist and CGSO Chief (i) utilize the different report generation facility of the inventory system and, in doing so, provide accurate information (quantity and expiry date) into the system to generate reliable and useful reports; (ii) immediately encode into the system acquired medicines to facilitate systems update; (iii) keep track of the medicines distributed during medical missions so that these are properly accounted for in the system; and (iv) perform regular stock-taking in accordance with the said Manual on the NGAS, submit the required RPCI and adopt FEFO method to ensure that medicines are issued to clients before they expire; (b) CGSO Chief and Infotech Division Chief consider placing additional terminals with printer scanner in the Pharmacy to render faster delivery of service to constituents and, at the same time,  provide additional computer that personnel can use in monitoring inventory movement; and (c) Pharmacist inform the RHU and CHCs about medicines nearing their expiry date so that these are distributed to those in need before they actually expire to avoid unwarranted loss or wastage.

We recommended and the LCE is amenable to require the (a) BAC and its TWG to submit the deficient procurement documents as observed; and (b) City Accountant to (i) meticulously check the financial transactions and ensure that all required supporting documents are attached and necessary data/information are complete before payment; (ii) immediately furnish the Audit Team with the deficient documents and the  unsubmitted DVs charged under City Pharmacy with their supporting documents; and (iii) strictly comply with the submission of DVs and supporting documents within the reglementary period as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2009-006.
We recommended and LCE is amenable that the City Accountant and the City Treasurer remit intact all taxes withheld within the prescribed period.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that HDMF monthly premiums should be continuously deducted by the AAO from HRMO due from employees and fully remitted to the HDMF by the City Treasurer within the reglementary period set by the said law.
We recommended and the LCE concurred that the AAO from HRMO continue to ensure the monthly withholding and timely remittance of premiums due to the PHIC by the City Treasurer.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the  (a) AAO from HRMO, City Accountant and Treasurer continue deducting and remitting GSIS premiums and amortizations within the prescribed timeline; (b) HRMO Chief communicate to CAO any changes made in the report it submits to the latter to avoid the occurrence of discrepancy in their respective records and the incurrence of over or underpayment; and (c) City Accountant coordinate with the HRMO with the end view of reporting only the correct amount of remittance in the GL.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the concerned CGT Officials (a) be informed of the reportorial and monitoring requirement as mentioned in COA Circular No. 2020-009, in relation to COA Circular No. 2014-002; (b) enhance the current monitoring summary list of donations with the end view of providing sufficient description of donations received, distributed and balances at any given period of time; and (c) keep track the remaining undistributed/unconsummated donated goods for them to be accounted, valued and recorded in the books or in registries after the lifting of the health quarantine.

We recommended  and the LCE concurred that (a) thru the City DRRMO Chief, (i) continue crafting relevant programs/projects/activities addressing the identified hazards of the CGT and accordingly implement the same on a timely manner as projected in the plan; and (ii) greatly consider devising PPAs that will be centered on COVID-19 mitigation and prevention; (b)  concerned City Officials, particularly the BAC, submit the lacking documents to the Audit Team and observe compliance with the provisions of GPPB Circular in respect to the emergency procurement of goods and provisions of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR; and (c) BAC and the TWG meticulously examine the submitted bidding documents of the prospective contractors during the evaluation of their bids.

We recommended and LCE agreed that the (a) City Accountant submit immediately the DVs with their supporting documents pertaining to the noted transactions; and (b) strictly comply with the submission of DVs and supporting documents within the reglementary period as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the (a) BAC and its Secretariat (i) submit the lacking documents noted in the BGCM fund utilization to the Audit Team; and (ii) faithfully adhere to GPPB Circulars and other relevant laws, rules and regulations specifically in the emergency procurement of goods; (b) City Social Welfare and Development (CSWD) Officer  provide the list of recipients to the Audit Team for the 6,890 packs of relief goods earmarked for Barangays Balibago 1st (801 pax), Maligaya (1,779 pax) and Mayor’s Office (4,310 pax); and (c) account for the shortage of 849 relief goods distributed to various barangays.

members of the GFPS:

Continue the meetings regarding the options on the updating of the GAD database such as the (1) CBMS plan to roll-out their updating/survey in sync with the Philippine Statistics Authority (PSA) which will conduct the same in 2022, although efforts are being made for the possibility of an earlier roll-out with PSA this 2021. (2) Parallel effort being done by the GFPS to update the existing GAD database by requesting all LGUs within the province to submit their respective GAD databanks/records for subsequent consolidation  

b. While there were some programmed GAD programs, projects and activities (PPAs) that were not implemented or only partially implemented due to lack of funds or budgetary and legislative constraints, or by changes in target beneficiaries in response to the COVID 19 pandemic, the PGT was still able to deliver several PPAs, benefitting not only women but children as well.

The GFPS has also taken steps in strengthening its efforts on the monitoring and evaluation of the implementation of various GAD -related PPAs thru the GAD M&E Team by requiring all concerned offices and departments to submit periodic reports during the quarterly GFPS meetings, ensuring that PPAs are being implemented on a timely and efficient manner, in accordance with their target schedules and budget allocations.



i) The GFPS hereby commits to adhere to the provisions satated under Section 6 of PCW-DILG-NEDA-DBM JMCNo. 2016-01, and shall imform the DILG RIII in writing should there be any amendments in the GPB previously endorsed to and approved by the DILG RIII, whether there is a need to implement additional PPAs or any adjustment on GAD-related allocations, for a more responsive and balanced GAD budget and GAD PPAs.


ii) The GFPS, at present, has limited knowledge on Gender Analysis using the HGDG tool since they only had basic training on the said tool, which normally requires a two or three-day formal training to be facilitated by resource persons duly accredited RGADC III.. In this regard, we will be requesting for the conduct of training exercises that will focus on the HDDG tool for GFPS and GAD focal persons of every Department/Office.

assign personnel within the City Accounting Office (CAO) for the timely preparation of the BRS to determine the reconciling items, and prepare adjusting entries, if warranted; and

request each depository bank for the prompt issuance of monthly bank statements to facilitate the preparation of BRS by the CAO, or secure snap shot copies immediately after the end of each month.

the City officials to consider the submission of request for write-off of account receivables and secure authority from the COA Office to validly derecognize the dormant recorded collectibles applying procedures under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 as regards to the deceased and unlocated former accountable personnel.

Property Offices of both the CGT and the DepEd City Division Office build concrete coordination, strengthen the controls on property accountability and monitor fully the clearances on property of accountable DepEd personnel who are retiring, resigning or terminating their employment.

conduct regular review and reconciliation of reciprocal accounts and effect adjustments of reconciling items periodically, as identified and substantiated, to prevent existence of long outstanding unadjusted reconciling items.

We recommended that the LCE:
instruct the City Legal Officer to study the lease contracts and evaluate their surrounding circumstances including the propriety and implications of their pre-termination and/or to immediately address the widening disparity between the rental fees under the existing contracts and the valuation prevailing at present conditions;  and
through coordination with the CGS Officer, make representation to the Sanggunian Panglungsod to draft and enact a legislation establishing guidelines on leasing out of the City’s building premises and spaces giving primordial consideration to the protection of the City’s interest.

We reiterated our recommendations that the LCE instruct:
the CAO and the GSO to  update their records on PPE accounts and reconcile them and continue to exert effort in retrieving prior year’s information in order to bring the control accounts and the supporting ledgers into agreement;
the GSO to prepare inventory of PPE that are classified according to its proper account name, fund and location; and
the CAO to (i) compute and recognize depreciation expense for all the depreciable assets of the CGT in order to have a reliable and accurate presentation of balances of each PPE accounts in the financial statements; (ii) analyze the PPE accounts to investigate, identify, and clear the reconciling items; and (iii) prepare the necessary journal entries to adjust the duly verified reconciling items in order to reflect the correct PPE account balances.

We recommended that the Local Finance Committee, City Treasurer and the City Accountant continuously observe the faithful compliance with the terms and conditions of the contract of loans and ensure that the budgeting, cash allocation, and payments of the current loan obligations are considered in its annual fiscal planning and management.

We recommended that the:
Local School Board consider establishing an internal policy or agreement on managing SEF property to ensure that the accountability over issued property items is ascertained at any time  and  the existence and condition of the PPE items are reasonably verified periodically;
CGSO study the possibility of recommending to the LCE and Sanggunian Panglungsod the transfer, by way of donation, to the DepEd City School Division, the SEF-funded properties which can be managed and controlled directly by the DepEd officials saving the responsibility and accountability on any loss or wastage; and
Property Offices of both the CGT and the DepEd City Division Office build concrete coordination, strengthen the controls on property accountability and monitor fully the clearances on property of accountable DepEd personnel who are retiring, resigning or terminating their employment.

We recommended the LCE require the City Accountant to (a) reconstruct and update the subsidiary records of the CIP accounts; and (b) draw the appropriate reclassifying entry for completed projects based on information to be supplied by the City Engineering and adjusting entry for depreciation that had accumulated counting from the date of completion.
