observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE:

instruct responsible officials to ensure faithful compliance in any contract  where the City Government is a party;

City Agriculturist to conduct monitoring activities and retain appropriate reports therein; and

secure the approval of the funding agency in established guidelines on the selection of NGA funded projects and for the replacement of beneficiaries to ensure that the intended recipients will be benefited from the programs.

We recommended that the Local School Board (a) ensure the full or optimum utilization of the SEF during the budget year and observe prudence in overseeing the timely implementation of PPAs; and (b) commit that only PPAs allowable under the prevailing law and regulations are included.

We recommended that the LSB (a) follow and perform the activities in  preparing and approving the SEF Budget with indicative timelines as prescribed by DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1; and (b) comply on the submission of the required reports to appropriate offices.

We recommended that the CGT continue to withheld and remit the mandatory contributions and loan amortizations to GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PHIC.

We further recommended that the LCE instruct the Accounting Office to (a) conduct monthly reconciliation between the recognized withheld amounts and the remittances made and settle the discrepancies noted in the audit including the dormancy of unremitted trust obligations;  and (b) provide the corresponding schedules or subsidiary records for the periodic balances of the Due to GSIS/Pag-IBIG/PHIC accounts.

We recommended the LCE require the City Accountant to (a) reconstruct and update the subsidiary records of the CIP accounts; and (b) draw the appropriate reclassifying entry for completed projects based on information to be supplied by the City Engineering and adjusting entry for depreciation that had accumulated counting from the date of completion.

We reiterated our recommendations that that LCE:
instruct the Accounting Office to prepare BRS for all bank accounts of the City in order to detect any error and irregularity and make appropriate adjustment for deficiencies noted in the audit; and
direct the Treasurer and Accounting Office to (i) properly recognize the unreleased/unissued checks at the end of the accounting period as Accounts Payable, to reflect correct balances of the Cash in Bank; and (ii) reconcile records with the LBP in coordination with the CCEDO pertaining to Balik Sigla program.

We recommended that the City Accountant (a) instruct the responsible staff to ensure the proper accounting of PCF transactions particularly the establishment, replenishments and liquidation; and (b) make necessary adjusting entries to correct the affected accounts.

We recommended that the (a) LCE require from the accountable officers the immediate liquidation of their overdue cash advances; and (b) City Accountant to instruct the concerned staff to (i) prepare monthly reconciliation of the SLs and GLs and review the previous records to settle the difference noted; and (ii) prepare adjusting entries to correct the erroneous recording of the PCF transactions.

We recommended that the City Accountant (a) make an adjusting entry to correct the balance of the unexpended/unobligated balance of DRRM reported in the FS; (b) coordinate with the Treasury Office in order to fix the correct amount to be transferred; and (c) effect the transfer of the procured rescue vehicle from TF to GF.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the:
LFC to ensure that all valid expenditures related to loans are backed up with sufficient appropriation; and
City Accountant to (i) recognize the year-end accrual of interests pertaining to loans; (ii) utilize the appropriate account titles for the charges thereof; (iii) make necessary adjustments to correct the accounts affected; and (iv) present the Loans Payable as Non-Current Liabilities.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the City Accountant and Treasurer to collect from the concerned employee and retired officials and employees the balance of the subject Due from Officers and Employees account. We further recommended that the Accounting Office (a) stop the practice of remitting amounts to the BIR without holding the same amount first from the salaries of employees; (b) compute for any tax payable or refundable of retirees before releasing final payment to them; (c) check the computation of taxes withheld of each employee in the 15th and month end payrolls to avoid accumulation of errors; and (d) assign a staff to review or check the computation of the year-end tax refundable and payable of the employees.

We recommended that the Management submit  (a) proof of compliance with Congress Joint Resolution No. 4 and SP ordinance/ resolution on the grant of allowances for DepEd personnel; and (b) guidelines on the grant of the teachers’ allowance in case of compliance with JR No. 4.  Likewise, we recommended the submission of (i) authorization letters of those teachers whose allowances were received by other persons or certification that they acknowledged and actually received the amount due them; and (ii) justification of irregular occurrence of Division Superintendent’s signature on the Obligation Request for General Fund account.
We recommended that Management (a) formulate guidelines for the conduct of TBA activities that would provide relevant, responsive, and rational set standards or parameters in the implementation of team building activities; and (b) justify the (i) conduct of TBA even if the City had issue on funding due to cash deficit; and (ii) observations presented above.
We recommended that the LCE instruct the City Treasurer and Accountant to continue to strengthen the internal controls over cash by subjecting all compensation payments through ATM facility.

We recommended that the LCE (a) ensure that all land where the infrastructure were proposed to be constructed are government properties to avoid delay in the realization of the projects; and (b) continue to invest in high–impact or developmental projects for inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan.
We further recommended that the LCE instruct the BAC and its Secretariat, CGSO, CEO and the Accounting Office to submit the lacking documents and submit explanations on the other issues stated.

We reiterated the previous recommendation that the LCE and the City Accountant seriously address the persisting issue on the delay of submission of year-end financial statements.  Moreover, we recommended the immediate submission of the year-end financial reports to the Audit Team in order to complete the desired audit actions and to render opinion thereon.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the City Accountant and responsible process owners in the preparation and submission of records and reports to improve further the compliance on the transmission of the financial information and accounts to the Auditor.

We recommended that the (a) CGT continue to allocate the mandatory appropriation or funding for LCPC; and (b) LCE direct the BAC and its Secretariat to submit explanation/documents/information for the post audit findings indicated above.

We recommended that the LCE:
instruct responsible officials to ensure faithful compliance in any contract  where the City Government is a party;
City Agriculturist to conduct monitoring activities and retain appropriate reports therein; and
secure the approval of the funding agency in established guidelines on the selection of NGA funded projects and for the replacement of beneficiaries to ensure that the intended recipients will be benefited from the programs.

We recommended that the Local School Board (a) ensure the full or optimum utilization of the SEF during the budget year and observe prudence in overseeing the timely implementation of PPAs; and (b) commit that only PPAs allowable under the prevailing law and regulations are included.

We recommended that the LSB (a) follow and perform the activities in  preparing and approving the SEF Budget with indicative timelines as prescribed by DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1; and (b) comply on the submission of the required reports to appropriate offices.

We recommended that the CGT continue to withheld and remit the mandatory contributions and loan amortizations to GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PHIC.
We further recommended that the LCE instruct the Accounting Office to (a) conduct monthly reconciliation between the recognized withheld amounts and the remittances made and settle the discrepancies noted in the audit including the dormancy of unremitted trust obligations;  and (b) provide the corresponding schedules or subsidiary records for the periodic balances of the Due to GSIS/Pag-IBIG/PHIC accounts.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the CGT continue to withheld taxes from its employees’ salaries and from payments to suppliers and remit the same to the BIR on or before the 10th day of the following month. We likewise recommend that the LCE direct the Accounting and Treasury Offices to prepare remittance schedules based on the actual recognized trust liabilities, conduct periodic reconciliations of their records, and provide schedules or subsidiary records for unremitted balances.

No single consolidated recommendation statement for the entire Statement of Audit Suspensions, Disallowances and Charges was explicitly provided beyond the individual recommendations already included in the respective observations above.

We recommended that:

(a)	the City Accountant designate personnel who shall be responsible in (i) monitoring the timely liquidation of cash advances and informing/reminding accountable officer of the due dates for liquidation; (ii) determining the physical status (whether living or not) and locating the whereabouts of retired/resigned accountable officers with unliquidated cash advance; (iii) preparing and serving demand letters to every accountable officer located; (iv) searching for the initial disbursement vouchers granting the cash advances to accountable officers; (v) gathering evidences such as death certificate, clearance from money and property accountability, and previous demand letters issued – all intended to support further request for write-off of unliquidated cash advances; (vi) preparing a Status Report for submission to the City Accountant, suggesting therein the appropriate action to be undertaken such as recommending the request for write-off of the unsettled cash advances, or endorsing the case to the City Legal Office for appropriate legal action; and (vii) coordinating with the Auditor for purposes of endorsing the case to the Civil Service Commission and the Ombudsman for further legal proceedings; and

(b)	the City Mayor (i) require the City Accountant, the City Treasurer, the City Administrator, and the immediate supervisor of the concerned accountable officers to defer signing on the clearance from money and property accountability until the unliquidated cash advance of the concerned accountable officer requesting said clearance is fully settled; and (ii) consider requesting the inclusion of the City Accountant as signatory in the clearance from money and property accountability of the City Schools Division Office for purposes of preventing any accountable officer from leaving the government service without fully liquidating his/her cash advance with the City Government of Tarlac.

We recommended that:

(a)	the City Mayor ensure the submission by the City Accountant of all disbursement vouchers for the confidential/intelligence cash advance with complete required supporting documents to the ATL within seven working days from release of the check, as well as the liquidation thereof in accordance with Section 6.2 of the Joint Circular; and

(b)	the City Accountant submit all lacking documents to prevent the issuance of a Notice of Suspension.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendations that:

(a)	the City Mayor constitute an Inventory Team that will be responsible for the execution of regular stock-take of the agency’s inventories; and

(b)	the City Accountant take up in the books of accounts the drugs and medicines amounting to P3,536,190.00 which remained unutilized at year-end.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendations that:

(a)	 the City Accountant  require the concerned Accounting staff to exert extra effort in accounting for the prior years’ PPE ledger balances of P2.155 billion by retrieving previous years’ records kept in the Accounting Office and update the current year’s subsidiary ledgers ensuring that all asset acquisitions and dispositions made in CY 2015 are properly and correctly recorded; and

(b)	 the CGSO perform annual physical count of PPE and submit copy of the report on the results thereof to the COA and the Accounting Office for information, reference and reporting purposes.

We recommended that:

(a)	the City Mayor and other concerned officials consider adopting a decentralized solid waste management system by providing financial and technical assistance for the establishment and operation of functional and standard-based MRFs in all the 76 barangays of Tarlac City; and

(b)	the CENRO design more effective strategies that are intended to further educate the public about the benefits of adopting proper solid waste segregation.

We recommended that the members of the Program Management Committee headed by the City Mayor (i) consider transferring to CCEDO the over-all management of the “BALIK SIGLA Program” in light of the shortcomings showcased by the LBP in handling the Program’s Fund and in order to save the City Government from shelling out management fee; (ii) initiate collection suit to recover the P4,370,128.99 funds lent to 30 defunct cooperatives; and (iii) consider allocating, and not merely appropriating funds to the Program in order to sustain its operation and continue providing assistance to eligible cooperatives.

We recommended that:

(a)	the City Mayor (i) immediately submit letter request for installment payment to the concerned office of the Commission on Audit and furnish the Audit Team Leader a copy of said letter request duly received by the proper COA office; and (ii) continue to settle the remaining disallowance; and

(b)	the Bids and Awards Committee ( ) Secretariat, members of the Technical Working Group (TWG), and other concerned offices submit to the Audit Team Leader the un-submitted supporting documents within the remaining number of days specified above in order to prevent the Notices of Suspension from maturing into disallowance.

We recommended that the CGT continue managing its finances under its GF and TF to prevent the incurrence of cash deficit. We further recommend that the Local School Board revisit its budgeting, revenues, expenditures and disbursements for it to be able to apply remedies on the deficit incurred, like strengthening its collection strategies. We likewise recommend that the Accounting Office adjust its records regarding the understated income and cash accounts and to properly recognize advance payments in the books of accounts.
The Accounting Office informed the Audit Team that the errors will be adjusted in the month of June 2017.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the CTO and the Accounting Office to stop the practice of recording unreleased/ unissued checks as if fully disbursed at the end of the accounting period, to reflect the factual status and correct balances of the Cash in Bank. Recording of check disbursements in the accounting books should be based on the Treasurer’s Reports of Checks Issued.  The Report shall be the basis for the preparation of the Journal Entry Voucher by the Accountant.
We further recommended that the CTO exert effort in contacting the payee-suppliers for the immediate release of their checks.
The Accounting Office apprised the Audit Team that all unreleased checks will be reverted to Cash in Bank next year-end.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Accounting Office to prepare BRS for all the bank accounts of the City, to detect any error and irregularity and be able to immediately correct the same and to present fairly the financial status of the LGU.
The City Accountant stated that he had already started evaluating the current manpower complement of the Accounting Office.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the CCEDO, CTO and the Accounting Office to coordinate with the LBP to correct and update their record.
We also reiterated the audit recommendations that the CGT - a.) consider transferring to CCEDO the over-all management of the Balik Sigla Program in order to save the CGT incurring management fee and for the easier documentation, recording and reporting of transactions; b.) institute legal actions to collect the receivables from the 30 defunct cooperative-borrowers; and c.) allocate funds to the program in order to sustain its operation and continue providing assistance to eligible cooperatives.
The LCE had instructed the City Accountant to look into the issue.

We recommend that the LCE:
Instruct the CAO, the Accounting Office and other concerned offices to revisit the project designs of the animal dispersals of the CGT and revise (e.g. when and to whom to pay in case the animal died, preparation, maintenance and submission of reports, facility or housing of returned offsprings, etc.) for any future implementation of the same project to properly recognize each transaction in the books of accounts and to attain goals and objectives;
Instruct the CAO to implement stipulations on returns of offspring and repayments; and
Instruct the CAO to prepare and submit status report of the livestock dispersal projects to the Accounting Office for the proper corrections of the financial records.
Investigate discrepancies in quantity between the purchased goats per disbursement voucher and recorded recipients per furnished list of the CAO.
For the recorded almost 100% mortality rate of goats, we further recommend that the services of the City Veterinary Office be tapped at the onset of any dispersal project. Deliveries of stock should be coordinated with the City Veterinarian for the early detection and prevention of diseases and infections. Also, monitoring of the project should be harmonized with the City Veterinary Office activities for the proper treatment of illnesses.
The LCE had instructed the CGSO to prepare a facility for the returns of animals by the beneficiaries. She also agreed that she will instruct the Head of the CAO to account for all the dispersal projects.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Accounting Office to compute and recognize depreciation expenses for all the depreciable assets of the CGT.
The City Accountant agreed to implement the audit recommendation.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the City Planning and Development, CTO and Accounting Offices, together with the CGSO to determine the status of the four resettlement sites of the City, establish a record of the beneficiaries, locate contracts between the City/Municipality of Tarlac and the recipients, to correct the books of accounts for the legal transfers of land to the beneficiaries and to record possible receivables and to design and implement strategies on the collection of the same.
The LCE stated that she already instructed concerned personnel to investigate on the matter.

We recommended that the LCE:
initiate dialogue with the Provincial Government regarding the collection of amusement tax, and the other issues contained in the Sangguniang Panlalawigan Resolution No. 089-2002, and settle the same; and
instruct the City Treasurer to enforce the provided administrative procedure on the collection of amusement tax.
The LCE informed the Audit Team that they already have three meetings regarding the issue and in February 2017, she sent a  group of personnel to monitor the operations of cockpits within the City and that the Management already discussed the crafting of a new tax code to address the issue.
The LCE further stated that she already instructed the City Human Resources Management Office (CHRMO) to create a task force for the monitoring of cockpit operations.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the CTO, Accounting Office, CGSO, the BAC and its Secretariat and Technical Working Group (TWG) and other concerned offices to submit the relevant documents supporting the recorded additional loans payable and the alleged utilization of the same which was the procurement of various heavy equipment.
The incumbent City Mayor explained that the subject loan was availed by the previous administration. She further explained that she only found out about the loan from the LBP when officials of the bank asked her if she was aware of such two months after her assumption of office. Her administration validated the transaction and found out that indeed there was a granted loan and the procured heavy equipments out of the proceeds of the loan were still in the pier since they were imported. Inasmuch as the loan was already incurring interest they decided to receive the said properties.
The City Administrator explained that the previous administration claims that the supplier of the heavy equipment is a sole distributor but they were not able to present any document pertaining thereto.
The City Mayor agreed to instruct the responsible offices and employees to submit relevant documents.

We reiterated the previous recommendations that the LCE and the City Accountant seriously address the persisting issue on the delay in the submission of mandatory reports to COA by requiring that only those disbursement vouchers with complete supporting documents are processed and paid.
The LCE and Accountant agreed to implement the audit recommendations.

We reiterated the audit recommendation that the LCE ensure the submission by the City Accountant of all disbursement vouchers for confidential cash advance, with complete required supporting documents in a sealed envelope to the ATL within seven working days from release of the check, as well as the liquidation thereof in accordance with Section 6.2 of the Joint Circular. We further recommended that the lacking document/s be submitted to prevent the issuance of a Notice of Suspension. Moreover, we recommended that the City Mayor see to it that her cash advance of P1 million should not used for reimbursement of any expenses.
The LCE and Accountant agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) direct the City Planning and Development Coordinator to monitor the effective and efficient implementation of the projects under the 20% Development Fund so that necessary interventions by the implementing offices may be made for their timely completion; (b) ensure that all land where the infrastructures  are proposed to be constructed are government properties to avoid delay in the realization of the projects; and (c) continue to invest in high–impact or developmental projects for inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan.
The LCE apprised the Audit Team that they are very cautious now in the implementation of projects. They are checking if a project can be funded or there is already an implementation in-process by national government agencies.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the BAC and its Secretariat and TWG, CGSO, CEO and the Accounting Office to submit the lacking documents and submit explanations on the other issues stated.
The LCE stated that she will instruct the concerned employees.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Accounting and Treasury Offices, the BAC and its Secretariat and TWG, and other concerned offices and personnel to process the payment of the subject projects and submit to the Audit Team the corresponding disbursement vouchers and supporting documents.
The City Accountant explained that seven out of the eight projects were already paid as of February 27, 2017 and the corresponding balances were already refunded to the source agencies.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the BAC and its Secretariat and TWG, the Accounting Office and the CEO to submit the lacking documents.
The LCE concurred with the Audit Team’s recommendation.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the offices responsible for the preparation of projects proposals and its implementation to coordinate with the source agencies for the immediate and proper implementation of the projects.
The DILG Officer assigned to the CGT informed the Audit Team that the ₱1.2 million from the DILG was for the extension of the evacuation center constructed in Brgy. Baras-Baras and it was already implemented.
The Audit Team averred that based on the review of financial reports, there were no disbursements yet from the DILG P1.2 million fund.
The City Agriculturist and the OIC of the CSWDO also informed the Audit team that the Organic Fertilizer Production and the Sustainable Livelihood Program, respectively, are already being implemented (2017).
The Audit Team requested status report of the said projects for validation purposes.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the concerned offices to establish guidelines on the selection of NGA funded projects. The established guidelines should be approved and concurred by the funding agencies.
The LCE explained that the beneficiaries were replaced because they were already grantees of other projects.
The Management also agreed to provide the Audit Team a copy of the DA approval regarding the change of beneficiaries.
Likewise, the DILG Officer also explained that the LPRAT prepared a resolution for the change of the beneficiaries.

We recommended that the City continue implementing PPAs under the SEF. We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Accounting, Treasury, Engineering Offices, the BAC and its Secretariat and other concerned offices to be aware of the issues observed and henceforth, prevent the same. Further, we recommended that the same offices, including the accountable officer for the ₱4,970,200.00, submit the lacking documents and explain on the other observations.
The LCE explained that she already discussed with the Local School Board that the plans for the 2018 CLARAA should be submitted in August 2017 for the proper procurement of the necessary equipment, supplies and materials.

We recommended that the LCE –
instruct the CGSO to issue procured properties from the SEF fund to end users and issue individual ARE. The said properties should also be tagged with property numbers and the accountable officers be instructed to turn-over to the CGSO the issued properties upon transfer to other school/division/town; and
request the DepEd to inform the CGT for every movement of DepEd personnel assigned in the City for the CGSO to be able to monitor the proper turn-over of issued properties to the employees who will be moving out from a certain school or the City Schools Division.
The LCE queried if the purchased properties out of the SEF are properties of the CGT. The Audit Team replied that since it is the City who procured said items, such are properties of the LGU.
The Executive Assistant of the Vice-Mayor also informed the Audit Team that based on their verification there were still 35 laptops in the possession of the DepEd Superintendent.
The LCE agreed to coordinate with the DepEd regarding the issuance of properties and the turnover of the same to the City by the accountable officers upon transfer, resignation or retirement.

We recommended that the City Mayor and the Sangguniang Panglungsod discuss the City’s compliance with R. A. No. 10121, specifically on the required members of the LDRRMC, to prevent any issue on the carrying out of the Council’s powers and functions.
The LCE agreed to comply with the audit recommendation.

We recommended that the City Mayor as the Chairperson of the TCDRRMC cause the holding of quarterly convention of the body for them to tackle issues on disaster risk management and to improve the preparedness and response capacity of the City.
The LCE explained that she is not yet aware of the said quarterly meeting, since she assumed office in July 2016 only, but she assured the Audit Team that the audit recommendation will be complied with in CY 2017.
The OIC-TDRRMO also informed the Audit Team that the first meeting was already scheduled on March 7, 2017.

We recommended that the Human and Resource Management Office (HRMO) facilitate the hiring of TCDRRMO personnel.
The Officer-in-Charge of the CHRMO agreed to implement the audit recommendation.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the TCDRRMO and TCDRRMC to prepare, submit and monitor a LDRRMFIP for the proper and timely implementation of projects, programs and activities regarding DRRM.
The LCE agreed to implement the audit recommendation.

We recommended that the LCE - 1.) instruct the TCDRRMO to coordinate with the Property Office regarding the recording, property tagging and maintenance of equipment ; 2.) include in the City’s priority programs the renovation of the office of the TCDRRMO for the proper maintenance of the respond and rescue equipment; 3.) evaluate the necessity of the dump and boom trucks; 4.) instruct the vehicle pre and post repair inspector to indicate the plate numbers of the vehicles in his report and instruct the Property Office to maintain vehicle ledgers to monitor their maintenance expenses;  and 5.) approve purchase requests to be charged against the calamity fund only if the necessity was justified by the TCDRRMO and in accordance with the LDRRMFIP.
The LCE averred that they were in the process of looking into the needs of the TCDRRMO.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the BAC and its Secretariat, TWG, CEO, CGSO and Accounting Office to provide the lacking supporting documents and submit explanations on the other issues stated.
The LCE explained that almost all of the accountable officers and employees are not connected with the City anymore but assured the Audit Team that her administration will find ways to implement the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Accounting Office adjust the books of accounts for the issued response and rescue equipment procured out of the Trust Fund – LDRRMF and to submit monthly the report on LDRRMF utilization.
The City Accountant agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the LCE undertake the necessary actions to encourage and/or request women representatives from the NGOs/CSOs members in the local development councils to achieve the goal of having an equitable representation of women in all spheres of society particularly in the decision-making and policy-making processes in the government and to fully realize their role as agents of development.
The LCE agreed to implement the audit recommendation.
Further, the following were the stated activities under the GAD:
Celebration of Women’s Month in March 2017;
Giving of prosthetic legs;
Giving of hearing aide (soon); and
Improvement of building ramps for PWD.

We recommended that the GFPS orient the Barangay VAW Desk officers on the preparation and submission of quarterly reports to the DILG and the CSWDO for the latter to take appropriate action based on the reports. We further recommended that the City GFPS coordinate with other competent agencies on the conduct of formal training of the Barangay VAW Desk Officers.
The LCE and the DILG officer informed the Audit Team that they will be conducting a seminar/training of barangay officials in March 2017 and one of the topics will be the VAWC.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System initiate the revision of the City GAD Code/Ordinance and comply with PCW Memorandum Circular 2009-1.
The LCE informed the Audit Team that the audit recommendation will be implemented by the CGT.
The Executive Assistant of the Vice-Mayor also concurred that the updating of the GAD Ordinance will be prioritized.

We recommended that the LCE formally reconstitute the CGT GFPS and capacitate, through seminars and trainings, the newly designated office heads.
The LCE agreed to comply with the audit recommendation.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the ESP Committee to strictly observe the qualifications set for the ESP. We also recommended the revisit of the guidelines to be able to update the amounts granted and the required grades of the scholars to be able to cope with the current condition of the economy.
The LCE and the Executive Assistant of the Vice-Mayor agreed to comply with the audit recommendation.
The LCE also explained that she had already planned the change of the required average of scholars, to wit:
Full Scholar		- 1.00 to 2.00
Partial Scholar		- 2.10 to 2.5
The LCE also announced that some Senators will be giving funds for the City’s Scholarship Programs
The Audit Team reminded the Management to consider the planned removal of tuition fees in state colleges and universities.

We recommended that the:
a. 	City Accountant monitor the liquidation of cash advance granted to accountable officers and demand immediate liquidation thereof in accordance with the timeline prescribed by COA;
b.	BAC, BAC Secretariat, and TWG submit the lacking documents enumerated in paragraph 31.4 to avoid the issuance of a Notice of Suspension; and
c.	SDO submit a certified copy of his official designation as SDO and his approved application for bond to avoid the issuance of a Notice of Disallowance.
The SDO had submitted his designation document signed by the former Municipal Mayor.

We recommended that the members of the SP consider enacting appropriate and comprehensive ordinance on the regulation of the operations of three-wheeled public conveyance along the highways within the City to prevent undesirable outcomes which may pose hazards and imminent danger to pedestrians and motorists’ lives and properties. We further recommended that the City initiate coordination with the PNP and LTO regarding the implementation of said Ordinance, especially on apprehending violators.
The LCE stated that, together with the Sangguniang Panglungsod, have reviewed the total traffic scheme within the City, and they are in process of addressing issues observed.
The Audit Team was also informed that the CGT is planning to install Close Circuit Televisions.

We recommended that the LCE together with the Human Resource Management Office prudently consider the above cited rules and regulations in the hiring of JOs; establish a clear cut internal policies thereto relative; and immediately terminate the contracts of JO employees whose functions are mere superfluous or duplications of the functions assigned to regular employees.
The LCE informed the Audit Team that they started evaluating the tasks assigned to JO employees and they already have initial observations that there are overlapping functions. They are planning to terminate 15 to 18 contracts but will hire four new contractual employees.

We recommended that the LCE and other concerned offices observe prudence and limitations in the utilization of resources intended for meals and snacks during meetings and other activities; and cause the inclusion of appropriation for Discretionary Fund in the Annual Budget of the City.
We further recommended that the LCE instruct the i.) Accounting and Budget Offices to recognize and charge, respectively, as representation expenses the disbursements for meals and snacks; and ii.) concerned offices to submit explanations on the issues observed in the post audit of the subjects expenses.
The LCE asked if they can provide meals and snacks in the conduct of trainings and seminars. The Audit Team informed her that as long as the provision of food is in the approved project design, it is really necessary and there is a budget for it, they incur such expense.

We recommended that the LCPC prepare a comprehensive plan to implement projects, programs and activities more relevant to the coverage of R.A. No. 9344.
We further recommended that the BAC and its Secretariat and TWG submit the lacking supporting documents for the procurement of the school kits.
We recommended that the LCPC prepare a comprehensive plan to implement projects, programs and activities more relevant to the coverage of R.A. No. 9344.
We further recommended that the BAC and its Secretariat and TWG submit the lacking supporting documents for the procurement of the school kits.

We recommended that the:
CHRMO and Accounting Office reconcile the source of the noted over-remittances, adjust records thereafter, and ensure to remit only the amount of recognized payables;
CHRMO ensure that remittance of premiums and loan amortizations to the GSIS are made within prescribed time to avoid payment of penalties; and
Concerned offices reconcile employees’ records with the GSIS to (i) verify that payments are made up-to-date; (ii) overpayments, if any, are refunded or applied to other outstanding loans of personnel with the GSIS; and (iii) invalid payables that do not have counterpart collectibles in the books of the GSIS are identified so that the same can be removed from the books of accounts.
We further recommended that the CGT consider the acquisition of a Payroll System to facilitate the preparation of payrolls and remittances schedules.
The Management agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommend that the LCE instruct concerned offices to comply with Revenue Regulation No. 2-28 dated April 7, 1998, as amended by Revenue Regulation Nos. 14-2002 and 10-2008 dated September 9, 2002 and July 8, 2008, respectively, in the withholding and timely remittance of taxes; and to exert more effort to determine the details of the beginning balance of the account Due to BIR.
The City Treasurer and the City Accountant agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that:

(a)	the City Mayor control the expenses of the agency (i) by seeing to it that the certification of the City Treasurer is affixed in the purchase request or its equivalent before the same is finally approved; and (ii) by deferring expenditures whenever funds are not available for said purpose; and

(b) the City Accounting Office continue to exercise its pre-audit function in order to ensure that only valid claims are recorded in the books of accounts of the agency.

We recommended that:

(a)	the City Treasurer and members of the Local Finance Committee (LFC) determine the income reasonably collectible for the ensuing year taking into consideration the collection performance in the previous years so that a repeat of bloated budget is prevented; and

(b)	the City Mayor and members of the LFC 
(i) monitor the actual collections and control expenditures in order to avoid another deficit in CY 2016; and (ii) propose and implement measures to further improve the collection performance of the agency so that programs, projects and activities pipelined in CY 2016 may not be compromised.

We recommended that:

(a)	the City Treasurer (i) continue to maintain an updated record of the checks released by the PTO for the CGT’s share on the tax on gravel and sand; and (ii) consider requesting for copy of computation sheet and/or delivery receipt in support to the remittances of the PTO; and

(b)	the City Accountant continue to recognize in the book of accounts the agency’s share from the tax on gravel and sand remitted by the PGT.

We recommended that:

(a)	the CAO (i) designate personnel who will be responsible for the timely preparation of disbursement voucher and routing thereof to the offices of the concerned officials whose signatures are required to facilitate the approval of the release of the barangays’ share; and (ii) forward the approved disbursement vouchers to the CTO at least two days after the end of the quarter period; and

(b)	the CTO (i) remind the CAO of its duty to facilitate the preparation and approval of the disbursement voucher for the release of the barangays’ share from real property tax collection; and (ii) prepare and deliver the checks covering the barangays’ share from the real property tax collection to the authorized government depository bank of the barangays on or before the fifth day of the month following the end of the quarter period.

We recommended that:

(a)	the CTO release the City aid to Barangay Care within five days after the end of each quarter, or even earlier, in compliance with Section 286 of the Local Government Code of 1991; and

(b)	the CBO inform the concerned barangay officials of Barangay Care about future increases of the City aid to the barangay and require the Barangay Treasurer to indicate the correct figure in the disbursement voucher covering the quarterly subsidy of the City to the barangay.

We recommended that the CGSO (i) comply with the rules requiring the availment of the services of the PS-DBM in the procurement of office supplies requirement of the various offices of the LGU; and (ii) require the concerned staff of the agency’s various offices to monitor the three-month office supply requirement of their respective offices and immediately request for replenishment when the stocks fall below the minimum threshold so that occurrence of emergency procurement is minimized, if not avoided.

We recommended that:

(a)	the SP members (i) continue to support the advocacy for good governance and effective legislation by joining the CLLEP sessions programmed by the PCL whenever budget is available therefor; and 
(ii) continue the practice of submitting on time the liquidation reports with complete supporting documents;
(b)	the Office of Councilor Mendoza provide an explanation in writing, stating therein the reason/s for failing to submit the liquidation report to the CAO within the prescribed period for settlement of accounts; and

(c)	the City Accountant (i) require the concerned accounting staff to transmit to COA the liquidation report submitted by the Office of Councilor Mendoza so that the liquidation report and supporting documents can be post-audited; and
 (ii) prepare correcting entries to rectify the double recording of accounts “Travelling Expenses” and “Training Expenses” by debiting the account “Advances to Officers and Employees” and crediting the account “Prior Year’s Adjustments” in the amount of P28,483.00.

We recommended that:

(a)	the concerned offices satisfy the audit issues concerning the eight transactions worth P9,868,240.00 presented in paragraph 13.2 by submitting the lacking of documents and requested written justification/explanation;

(b)	the City Accounting Office submit the disbursement vouchers for various payments worth P23,982,417.87 as discussed in paragraph 13.4; and

(c)	the CEO continue the good practice of monitoring the implementation and completion of projects intended to prevent/address the impact of calamities in the lives and properties of the City’s constituents.

We recommended that the City Treasurer immediately transfer the unutilized QRF and MF-MOOE of P2,459,558.50 to the special trust account to prevent its integration to the general fund of the agency and to avoid its use for purposes other than disaster-related activities.

We recommended that:

(a)	the Local School Board (i) utilize the Special Education Fund for priority projects, programs and activities prescribed by the Local Government Code; and (ii) continue to implement construction and/or repair of school buildings in order to address the facility requirements of public schools students; and

(b)	the BAC (i) continue to submit disbursement vouchers supported with complete documentation; and (ii) require the concerned contractors to submit warranty security for the goods they delivered.

We recommended that:

a.	the GFPS (i) consult first with their intended clients so that only relevant programs, projects and activities that best address the gender issues identified are programmed, budgeted and implemented; (ii) conduct post-implementation activities so that only truthful information relating to the actual results of programs and projects implemented are reported in the GAD Accomplishment Report; and (iii) consider the suggestions of the farmers and study the possibility of subsidizing more fertilizers, water pumps and sprayers to help increase their crop yield; and

b.	the City Agriculture Office conduct closing exercises in the five barangays where field production trainings were concluded and distribute training certificates to the farmer-participants.

We reiterated our previous recommendations that:

(a)	the City Mayor and the City Accountant seriously address the persisting issue on the delay in the submission of mandatory reports to COA by requiring that only those disbursement vouchers with complete supporting documents are processed and paid; and

(b)	the City Accountant (i) observe the due dates of submission of the required financial reports and documents to the Audit Team; and (ii) submit the remaining disbursement vouchers, reports of collections and deposits and trial balances for CY 2015 that have not yet been forwarded to the Audit Team.
